options: long
int
Compile error — ambiguous call
Runtime error
questions.explanation: The literal 5 is an int, so the compiler picks the exact match print(int). Widening to long would only happen if no int overload existed.

questions.question: Which statement about constructor chaining with `this()` is correct?
options: this() can appear anywhere in the constructor body
this() must be the first statement in the constructor
this() can be combined with super() in the same constructor
this() is optional — the compiler always inserts it
questions.explanation: A call to this() must be the first statement in a constructor. You cannot have both this() and super() in the same constructor.

questions.question:
What is wrong with this method?
```java
public void process(String... names, int count) { }
```
options: Varargs cannot be used with other parameters
Varargs parameter must be the last parameter
String... is not valid syntax
Nothing — this compiles fine
questions.explanation: A varargs parameter must be the last parameter in the method signature. The correct form would be process(int count, String... names).

questions.question: Can a static method access an instance field directly?
options: Yes — all methods can access all fields
Yes — but only if the field is public
No — static methods have no `this` reference to resolve instance fields
No — static methods can only call other static methods
questions.explanation: Static methods belong to the class and have no `this` reference. They can only access instance fields through an explicit object reference.

questions.question: What does the compiler generate if you write NO constructor in a class?
options: A private no-arg constructor
A public no-arg constructor that calls super()
Nothing — the class cannot be instantiated
A constructor matching the parent class constructors
questions.explanation: The compiler generates a default no-arg constructor with the same access as the class. It calls super() to invoke the parent's no-arg constructor.
